summary: Feature request to add X.509 digital signature support to PDF documents generated via SKDocument.CreatePdf(), allowing documents to be signed without requiring Adobe Reader.

analysis.summary: The reporter wants SkiaSharp to support embedding X.509 digital signatures (PKCS#7/CMS) into PDF documents at generation time. SkiaSharp's current PDF API (SKDocument) wraps Skia's SkPDF backend, which does not expose a digital signing hook in its C API surface. Upstream Skia does not natively support PDF digital signing; this feature would require either a post-processing step using a dedicated PDF library or upstream Skia changes.
analysis.rationale: This is a clearly stated feature request for new functionality that does not exist in SkiaSharp or upstream Skia. No broken behavior is described. The request is well-understood (PDF digital signatures via X.509/PKCS#7), but implementation requires either changes upstream or a post-processing approach external to SkiaSharp's PDF generation pipeline.

keySignals.text: Would be amazing if we can add x509Certificate2 signatures for PDF documents generated by SkDocument.CreatePdf()
keySignals.interpretation: Requesting new API functionality for digital PDF signing.

keySignals.text: I believe that using a new package just for this couldn't be good.
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter prefers integrated solution but is open to alternatives.

codeInvestigation.finding: SKDocument.CreatePdf() and its overloads with SKDocumentPdfMetadata wrap the Skia C API for PDF generation. No signing capability exists in the metadata struct or document API. The SKDocumentPdfMetadata only includes fTitle, fAuthor, fSubject, fKeywords, fCreator, fProducer, fRasterDPI, fPDFA, fEncodingQuality, fCreation, fModified — no signature or certificate fields.
SKDocumentPdfMetadata struct contains no certificate, signer, or signature-related fields. The entire PDF metadata surface has no hooks for digital signing.

workarounds: Use iTextSharp (LGPL) or PDFsharp to post-process the PDF output from SkiaSharp and embed X.509 digital signatures
Use PdfPig or Docnet.Core libraries which support digital signing as a post-processing step on existing PDFs
Use the PdfSharpCore NuGet package to sign PDFs generated by SkiaSharp after saving them
nextQuestions: Does upstream Skia's SkPDF backend have any hooks for signing that could be exposed via C API?
Would the reporter accept a post-processing workflow using an existing .NET PDF signing library?

resolution.hypothesis: PDF digital signing is not supported by upstream Skia's PDF backend. The feature would need to be implemented either by post-processing the PDF output with a dedicated library, or by exposing new Skia upstream signing infrastructure (if it exists) through the C API and C# bindings.

proposals.title: Post-process with iTextSharp or PDFsharp
proposals.description: Generate the PDF with SkiaSharp, then use iTextSharp (LGPL) or PDFsharp to digitally sign the output. Both libraries support X.509/PKCS#7 digital signatures on existing PDF files.

proposals.title: Implement via upstream Skia extension
proposals.description: Research if upstream Skia SkPDF supports a signing callback or hook, then expose it through the C API layer and C# bindings. This would be a significant multi-layer effort.

resolution.recommendedReason: The workaround is immediately actionable without waiting for SkiaSharp changes. PDF digital signing is a complex standard (PKCS#7/CMS) better handled by dedicated PDF libraries. SkiaSharp's PDF backend (Skia's SkPDF) does not natively support signing.

actionability.reason: Valid feature request with 3 upvotes. No upstream Skia support exists today, so this requires design discussion. A workaround exists using dedicated PDF signing libraries.

actions.description: Apply feature-request and PDF backend labels
Acknowledge request and suggest workaround using dedicated PDF signing library
actions.comment:
Thanks for the feature request! PDF digital signing (X.509/PKCS#7) is not currently supported by SkiaSharp or the underlying Skia PDF backend (`SkPDF`), so this would require significant upstream work.

**Workaround:** You can generate the PDF with SkiaSharp and then use a dedicated .NET PDF library to digitally sign it:

- **iTextSharp** (LGPL) – well-established, supports X.509 digital signatures via `PdfStamper`
- **PDFsharp** – open-source, supports PKCS#7 signing via `PdfDocumentSigner`
- **PdfPig** – modern .NET library with signing support

These libraries can open the PDF output stream from `SKDocument.CreatePdf()` and embed a digital signature using your `X509Certificate2` without needing to rerender the document.

We'll keep this open to track interest in native support.
